does: This paper introduces the CER framework, a diagnostic tool for assessing insurability of losses caused by generative and agentic AI systems. The framework comprises three components: Control Boundary examines whether the insured organization established enforceable operating envelopes for their AI system; Evidence Reconstruction determines whether system state, reasoning traces, and causal chains can be reconstructed from retained logs and artifacts; Insurance Response evaluates whether reconstructed losses fall within policy coverage. The paper addresses externally triggered failures including prompt injection, RAG poisoning, malicious tool output, credential misuse, and data poisoning, arguing that AI-mediated losses require state reconstruction rather than simple event reconstruction because the system's reasoning, retrieval, and tool use create complex causal chains distinct from traditional loss scenarios.
connects: This work builds on emerging intersections of AI governance, cybersecurity risk assessment, and insurance law. It extends prior work on AI safety boundaries and control problems by reframing them through an insurance and liability lens. The paper responds to growing recognition that standard insurance frameworks struggle with novel failure modes introduced by autonomous systems and RAG-based applications. It connects to work on AI system transparency and interpretability by treating log retention and artifact preservation as prerequisites for loss reconstruction. The framework also engages with existing security threat models in adversarial machine learning, particularly work on poisoning attacks and prompt injection, translating these technical concerns into risk transfer and claims documentation requirements.
enables: This framework enables insurers to develop underwriting criteria and claims procedures specific to AI systems, moving beyond denial or blanket exclusions toward risk-differentiated coverage. It provides organizations with concrete guidance on what control mechanisms, logging practices, and architectural decisions support insurable deployment of generative AI. The CER framework could facilitate development of AI-specific insurance products that incentivize better governance and observability. It opens pathways for establishing industry standards around artifact retention, causal chain documentation, and control envelope definition. Future work could formalize the framework into automated audit tools and develop benchmark datasets of reconstructed AI losses, enabling empirical study of which organizational practices most effectively support both loss prevention and claims recovery.
